page_content:
# Passage: 1
Perspective-taking and empathy are two related concepts. Each requires focusing on another person, yet perspective-taking is cognitive and empathy is affective. A group of researchers hypothesized that perspective-taking and empathy are two separate skills and designed two studies to test this hypothesis.  
Study 1 focused on perspective taking in a complex, strategic war game. The game was played by two participants against each other. The players could either “disarm” or “attack.” In the cooperation trials, a player won money if both parties disarmed and lost money if one party attacked. In the competition trials, a player won money by attacking and lost money by disarming, regardless of what the other player did. Success in the war game required a participant to infer the trial type and predict the other player’s strategy.  
Study 2 focused on empathy in a social coalition game. The participants were assigned to groups of three people (triads) and given a chance to get acquainted through sessions in which each triad discussed their home towns, favorite activities, and typical characteristics of their friends. After the getting-acquainted sessions, participants were told they would have a chance to form a coalition with one of the participants in their triad to participate in the next round and win money. For a coalition to be formed, two participants had to select each other independently. Success in the social coalition game was defined by two participants selecting one another, which required an understanding of how the other individuals in the triad felt about each other.  
At the start of both studies, the participants filled out four self-report questionnaires addressing the following: perspective-taking skills, empathy, personal distress when witnessing other people’s pain, and fantasy perspective taking (for example, getting really involved in the feelings and thoughts of characters in a novel). Higher scores on these questionnaires indicated higher levels of perspective-taking skills, empathy, personal distress, and fantasy perspective taking. The researchers’ purpose was to see if scores on these variables were associated with participants’ success rates in studies 1 and 2.
